question.prompt: What actions should the audit committee take based on these financial trends?

ground_truth.canonical_answer: Engage forensic accountants to review bill-and-hold and distributor transactions. Implement independent revenue cut-off testing. Review cash-to-revenue reconciliation monthly. Investigate distributor relationship for round-tripping.
key_facts: revenue recorded absent physical delivery or customer acceptance indicates premature recognition
structural cash conversion shortfall persists independent of seasonal and volume factors
DSO increased progressively from baseline levels through early signal to escalation, with the rate of deterioration accelerating over successive reporting periods rather than stabilizing
the revenue-cash divergence originates in premature revenue recognition practices, not inventory manipulation
the financial irregularity is concentrated in revenue recognition and AR aging, not in benefit obligations or pension accounting
correlation between revenue acceleration and aging-bucket shift rules out organic growth as explanation
seasonal sales push is not the explanation for AR growth
treasury cash management operations follow standard patterns, confirming the cash shortfall stems from receivables, not financing activities

answer.answer_text:
**Audit‑Committee Action Plan – What to do in response to the observed financial trends** | Observed trend (source) | Why it matters to the audit committee | Recommended actions (with citations) | |---|---|---| | **Rapid deterioration of AR aging and large write‑offs** – $4.2 M write‑off in Nov 2025; cumulative $34.2 M write‑off by Jun 2026; 120‑day‑plus AR rising from 11 % (Nov 2025) to >30 % (Jun 2026). | Indicates weakening credit controls, higher collection risk and potential mis‑statement of revenue. | • Demand a detailed **credit‑policy review** and **collection‑performance dashboard** from CFO.
• Require **weekly AR aging reports** and a **cash‑flow sensitivity analysis**.
• Mandate that the internal audit team **test a sample of high‑risk receivables** for recoverability.
• Escalate the issue to the **audit‑committee‑led remediation task force** (see the “CFO requests meeting with audit committee chair” in [financial_irregularity_02_ep_023]). | | **Material weakness in revenue‑recognition** – disclosed in Jun 2026; internal audit memo flagged procedures in Nov 2025; forensic review launched Jan 2026. | A material weakness can lead to misstated earnings and triggers SEC scrutiny. | • Order an **independent revenue‑recognition specialist** to assess the current policy and test a cross‑section of contracts (refer to the “material weakness disclosed” in [financial_irregularity_02_ep_030]).
• Require **management to produce a remediation plan** with milestones and to report progress at each audit‑committee meeting. | | **Bill‑and‑hold inventory aging** – $8.2 M (Nov 2025) and $14.7 M (Jan 2026) with oldest items >5 months. | Bill‑and‑hold arrangements are high‑risk for premature revenue recognition. | • Direct the **internal audit team to perform a physical verification** of all bill‑and‑hold inventory and confirm compliance with ASC 606.
• Ask the CFO to **re‑classify any non‑compliant items** and adjust revenue accordingly. | | **Cash‑conversion ratio collapsing** – 0.45 (Nov 2025) → 0.18 (Jun 2026). | Low conversion signals that operating cash is not keeping pace with earnings, increasing liquidity risk. | • Instruct finance to **re‑model working‑capital drivers** (AR, inventory, payables) and present a **cash‑improvement plan**.
• Consider **tightening payment terms** and **accelerating capex deferments** until cash conversion improves. | | **SEC informal inquiry & restatement risk** – SEC inquiry received Jan 2026; revenue restatement risk assessment underway; revised FY revenue cut from $780 M to $612 M (Jun 2026). | Regulatory exposure can lead to penalties, loss of investor confidence, and additional audit work. | • Retain **independent counsel** (already done in Jan 2026) and **task them with coordinating the SEC response**.
• Ask management to **prepare a draft restatement** and disclose material impacts to the board.
• Ensure the **external auditor expands its scope** to include the restatement areas. | | **Forensic review & independent counsel already engaged** – Jan 2026 forensic review per audit‑committee directive; external counsel engaged Jun 2026. | Shows the committee is already acting, but follow‑up is needed. | • Set **clear deadlines** for the forensic team’s final report and for counsel’s SEC response plan.
• Schedule a **joint audit‑committee/forensic‑team briefing** to translate findings into control‑remediation actions. | | **Headcount under budget & operational strain** – FTEs down 52 (Nov 2025) and 220 (Jun 2026). | Staffing cuts may have contributed to control breakdowns. | • Request a **risk‑based staffing assessment** to ensure critical audit‑function resources are not under‑resourced.
• If needed, **approve additional audit‑team hires** or outsource high‑risk testing. | | **External auditor notes material weakness & cumulative write‑offs** – Jun 2026 auditor notes “material weakness in revenue recognition” and “cumulative AR write‑off $34.2 M”. | Reinforces the need for heightened oversight. | • Ask the **external auditor to issue a formal Management Letter** with specific remediation recommendations.
• Require **management to sign off on each recommendation** and report status quarterly. | ### Consolidated Action Checklist for the Audit Committee 1. **Revenue‑Recognition Overhaul** – Engage an independent specialist, demand a remediation roadmap, and monitor implementation. 2. **AR & Collections Tightening** – Institute weekly aging dashboards, tighten credit limits, and test high‑risk receivables. 3. **Bill‑and‑Hold Compliance** – Conduct physical verification, re‑classify non‑compliant items, and adjust revenue. 4. **Cash‑Conversion Improvement** – Model working‑capital drivers, renegotiate payment terms, and defer non‑essential capex. 5. **Regulatory & Restatement Management** – Coordinate SEC response via counsel, prepare draft restatements, and expand external‑auditor scope. 6. **Forensic Review Follow‑Up** – Set deadlines for final forensic findings, translate them into control enhancements, and report progress. 7. **Resource Adequacy** – Review audit‑function staffing, approve needed hires or outsourcing to cover high‑risk areas. 8. **Enhanced Reporting** – Require quarterly updates from CFO, internal audit, forensic team, and external auditor on all above items. By acting on these points, the audit committee will directly address the deteriorating financial trends, mitigate the material weaknesses identified, and reduce the risk of regulatory penalties or further earnings restatements.

• Ask management to **prepare a draft restatement** and disclose material impacts to the board.
• Ensure the **external auditor expands its scope** to include the restatement areas. | | **Forensic review & independent counsel already engaged** – Jan 2026 forensic review per audit‑committee directive; external counsel engaged Jun 2026. | Shows the committee is already acting, but follow‑up is needed. | • Set **clear deadlines** for the forensic team’s final report and for counsel’s SEC response plan.
• Schedule a **joint audit‑committee/forensic‑team briefing** to translate findings into control‑remediation actions. | | **Headcount under budget & operational strain** – FTEs down 52 (Nov 2025) and 220 (Jun 2026). | Staffing cuts may have contributed to control breakdowns. | • Request a **risk‑based staffing assessment** to ensure critical audit‑function resources are not under‑resourced.
• If needed, **approve additional audit‑team hires** or outsource high‑risk testing. | | **External auditor notes material weakness & cumulative write‑offs** – Jun 2026 auditor notes “material weakness in revenue recognition” and “cumulative AR write‑off $34.2 M”. | Reinforces the need for heightened oversight. | • Ask the **external auditor to issue a formal Management Letter** with specific remediation recommendations.
• Require **management to sign off on each recommendation** and report status quarterly. | ### Consolidated Action Checklist for the Audit Committee 1. **Revenue‑Recognition Overhaul** – Engage an independent specialist, demand a remediation roadmap, and monitor implementation. 2. **AR & Collections Tightening** – Institute weekly aging dashboards, tighten credit limits, and test high‑risk receivables. 3. **Bill‑and‑Hold Compliance** – Conduct physical verification, re‑classify non‑compliant items, and adjust revenue. 4. **Cash‑Conversion Improvement** – Model working‑capital drivers, renegotiate payment terms, and defer non‑essential capex. 5. **Regulatory & Restatement Management** – Coordinate SEC response via counsel, prepare draft restatements, and expand external‑auditor scope. 6. **Forensic Review Follow‑Up** – Set deadlines for final forensic findings, translate them into control enhancements, and report progress. 7. **Resource Adequacy** – Review audit‑function staffing, approve needed hires or outsourcing to cover high‑risk areas. 8. **Enhanced Reporting** – Require quarterly updates from CFO, internal audit, forensic team, and external auditor on all above items. By acting on these points, the audit committee will directly address the deteriorating financial trends, mitigate the material weaknesses identified, and reduce the risk of regulatory penalties or further earnings restatements.
